Figure 2: Calibration under the null across varying levels of heteroskedastic noise. We simulated phenotypes from N = 10,000 individuals and M = 10,000 SNPs with five lifestyle environments under the null of no Gâ†’E variance (Ď‰2 gâ†’e = 0), varying noise-by-environment variance Ď‰2 nâ†’e â†‘ {0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08} across 200 replicates. A. Estimated Ď‰2 gâ†’e. Boxplots across 200 replicates; ENGINE is marked in blue and LEMMA in orange. B. Type I error. Figure 5: Ablation of cross-fitting (CV) and Ď–1 regularization on false-positive control. False positive rate under no true Gâ†’E as a function of heterogeneous noise Ď‰2 nâ†’e. Curves compare ENGINE with both safeguards (CV+Ď–1), without CV, without Ď–1, and with neither. The false postive rate is calculated as P(rejection at p < 0.05) across 100 replicates; error bars show 95% binomial CIs; the dashed line marks the significance level 0.05 (Ď–1 penalty Ď‚ = 5â†’ 10â†“3).

Figure 6: Lifestyle Gâ†’E for BMI: single environments vs. learned embedding. A. Singleexposure estimates. Estimates of Ď‰2 gâ†’e for each selected environmental exposure, with error bars indicating the estimated standard errors; the dashed vertical line marks the learned embedding estimate (Ď‰2 gâ†’e=2.34â†’ 10â†“2). Figure 1: Overview of ENGINE. A. Pipeline. Inputs are a genotype matrix, multiple environments, and a phenotype. ENGINE learns an interpretable environmental embedding e and reports point estimates and standard errors for the additive (Ď‰2 g), interaction (Ď‰2 gâ†’e), and noise-by-environment (Ď‰2 nâ†’e) variance components. For eâ€ťciency, genotype-dependent summaries (Ugâ†’e) are cached in a single streaming pass and reused across iterations. B. Separating heteroskedastic noise from Gâ†’E. With two environments, the combination that maximizes Nâ†’E variance can di!er from the one that maximizes Gâ†’E variance; ENGINE therefore learns weights that maximize Gâ†’E while controlling Nâ†’E. C. Interpretable environment weights. The entries of environmental weights (Ď‰) quantify the contribution of each environmental exposure to the embedding and to the captured Gâ†’E signal.

Figure 3: Power and estimation accuracy as a function of interaction strength. We simulated phenotypes from N = 10,000 individuals and M = 10,000 SNPs with five lifestyle environments, setting Ď‰2 g = 0.3, Ď‰2 gâ†’e â†‘ {0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25}, and Ď‰2 nâ†’e = Ď‰2 gâ†’e (200 replicates per setting). A. Variance estimates. Boxplots of Ď‰Ě‚2 g are in green and Ď‰Ě‚2 gâ†’e in blue across replicates; the horizontal dashed line marks zero. B. Power. Rejection rate at significance level 0.05 with 95% binomial confidence intervals. C. Embedding accuracy. Squared correlation between the learned environmental embedding and the ground truth.

Figure 4: Runtime benchmarks against alternative approaches. Points show mean wall-clock runtime over 200 replicates; error bars denote one standard deviation. Dotted curves are power-law fits over the observed range, and open markers at 100 k indicate values obtained by extrapolating these fits. Both axes are on a logarithmic scale. A. Scaling with sample size. The number of SNPs is fixed at M = 10,000 and we vary the number of individuals N â†‘ {1, 3, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100} k. The runtime for the exact-per-update ENGINE baseline at N = 100 k is extrapolated because the method did not complete within the 8-hour wall-clock limit under 30GB of memory. B. Scaling with SNP count. The number of individuals is fixed at N = 10,000 and we vary the number of SNPs M â†‘ {1, 3, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100} k. The runtime for the exact-per-update ENGINE baseline at M = 100 k is likewise extrapolated. All benchmarks were run on a single core of an Intel Xeon Gold 6140 CPU (36 cores, 2.30GHz) with a maximum of 30GB memory and an 8-hour wall-clock limit per replicate.
